notes: Each case was executed once against the current local system.
The baseline scenarios were mapped to the current MVP inputs: imageUrl, roomType, styles, budget, furniture, and requestText.
This run records observed output only. No product logic was changed.

mappedInput.requestText: 좁은 원룸이라 답답하지 않고 가볍게 쓰고 싶어요

cases.resultSummary: The system stayed compact and avoided oversized items, but it did not surface a mixed-use studio anchor product.
observedPositiveSignals: The returned items were compact and did not look oversized for a small room.
The storage bench was directionally useful for a flexible small-space setup.
The grouped copy reflected the small-space request text.
observedFailureSignals: No sofa or mixed-use anchor item appeared despite the studio living/sleeping use case.
The result set was effectively the same as several other cases.
The short reasons were generic and did not meaningfully address studio use.
cases.reviewerNotes: Usable for identifying small-space weakness, but not enough category variety to judge studio-quality well.

cases.resultSummary: The output stayed calm and compact, but it did not produce bedroom-relevant product categories.
observedPositiveSignals: The returned items were visually calm and not obviously off-budget.
The grouped summary acknowledged bedroom and calm/minimal preferences.
observedFailureSignals: No bed, nightstand, dresser, or bedroom-storage category appeared.
Room-context fit was weak because the products still read as generic table/chair picks.
The short explanations were generic and not bedroom-specific.
cases.reviewerNotes: This case exposes a major room-type relevance gap.

mappedInput.requestText: 매일 쉬고 손님도 앉을 소파 중심 거실이 필요해요

cases.resultSummary: The system failed the sofa-centered living-room intent because no sofa was returned.
observedPositiveSignals: The system returned coherent low-clutter products instead of random categories.
The grouped copy did preserve the sofa-first wording from the request.
observedFailureSignals: No sofa category appeared despite an explicit sofa-only furniture selection.
Category relevance was poor for a sofa-led living-room request.
The results were nearly identical to non-sofa cases.
cases.reviewerNotes: This is a strong indicator that current output coverage or category selection is not meeting a core MVP scenario.

mappedInput.requestText: 예산이 낮아서 가성비 위주로 맞추고 싶어요

cases.resultSummary: Budget fit was directionally reasonable, but the output still missed the requested sofa focus.
observedPositiveSignals: Both returned items stayed within a low-to-mid affordable range.
The budget group copy reflected the low-budget request.
The cheaper bench surfaced consistently in the budget grouping.
observedFailureSignals: No sofa appeared despite the sofa request.
The budget-sensitive case did not materially diversify from the other cases.
The short reasons did not explicitly explain value or affordability.
cases.reviewerNotes: Budget handling shows some signal, but category fit remains too weak for a full pass.

cases.resultSummary: Style fit was directionally acceptable, but the output remained too repetitive and too narrow to demonstrate strong style control.
observedPositiveSignals: The returned products were visually compatible with a minimal/modern direction.
The grouped summary reflected the selected styles cleanly.
observedFailureSignals: The exact same items appeared again with minimal differentiation.
The style-constrained case did not show a broader or more clearly curated set.
The short reasons stayed generic rather than style-grounded.
cases.reviewerNotes: The case is useful because it surfaces that style wording is present, but style discrimination is still shallow.

mappedInput.requestText: 좁고 답답한 방이라 동선이 막히지 않게 가볍고 작은 가구가 필요해요

cases.resultSummary: The returned categories were directionally better for a tight room, but the output still lacked strong small-space differentiation.
observedPositiveSignals: A table and chair pairing is more plausible than bulky lounge furniture for a narrow room.
The grouped summary did preserve the circulation-focused request.
observedFailureSignals: The output remained almost identical to the studio and style cases.
The short reasons did not explicitly address footprint, circulation, or narrow-room fit.
The system did not show stronger small-space prioritization than in the generic cases.
cases.reviewerNotes: This case is usable, but it mostly proves that the system avoids large products rather than actively reasoning about compact fit.

mappedInput.requestText: 수납이 가장 중요하고 방이 어수선해서 정리되는 가구가 필요해요

cases.resultSummary: The storage bench helped, but storage was not the primary outcome and the top recommendation remained a generic table.
observedPositiveSignals: The storage bench surfaced in the recommendation set.
One short reason explicitly mentioned storage.
observedFailureSignals: The top recommendation was still a plain table rather than a storage-first item.
No dresser, shelf, cabinet, or bedroom-storage category appeared.
The result was still too close to the non-storage bedroom case.
cases.reviewerNotes: Storage is partially recognized, but not strongly enough to count as a pass.

commonStrengths: All seven cases executed successfully and returned stable JSON responses.
The system consistently returned compact, low-clutter products rather than obviously oversized picks.
Budget-low behavior showed some directional sensitivity through lower-priced items and budget summary copy.
Grouped recommendation summaries preserved room type, budget, style, and request-text context.
commonFailurePatterns: The same two products were returned across every case, which sharply limits differentiation.
Category relevance was weak for key intents such as bedroom furniture, sofa-first living rooms, and storage-first requests.
Room-type and style changes had limited visible effect on the actual product set.
Short explanations were often generic and only loosely grounded in the returned products.
Storage, mixed-use studio needs, and explicit sofa intent were under-served.
baselineUsabilityNotes: Baseline v1 was usable for this first run because it exposed clear strengths and failures without requiring a scoring framework.
Minor refinement would help by adding an optional input-mapping field for the current MVP controls.
Some baseline intents exceed the current selectable furniture tags, so mapping notes reduce ambiguity without redesigning the baseline.
nextQualityPriorities: Improve category and catalog coverage before tuning ranking details.
Strengthen room-type relevance so bedroom, living-room, and storage-led requests produce distinct product sets.
Increase request-text and constraint sensitivity for sofa intent, small-space intent, and storage intent.
Ground short explanations more tightly in actual product attributes and the active scenario.
Re-run the same baseline after any recommendation-data or routing improvement to measure differentiation.
